worth_it_explanation: Magnite and the publisher's own ad server rarely report the same impression count for the same inventory, and a gap is expected. The audit value is in knowing whether that gap is measured and held inside a tolerance the publisher set on purpose, rather than left unwatched. A discrepancy that drifts past the accepted range points to lost revenue since the side that counts fewer impressions is usually the side that pays, so the check reads existing reports rather than rebuilding anything and the finding points at a concrete number to reconcile.
intro: A supply-side platform (SSP) such as Magnite and the publisher's own ad server each count impressions at a different point in the ad call, so the two numbers do not match exactly. This subcategory is about whether that difference is measured against a tolerance the publisher chose, and whether the publisher investigates the sources of the gap when it runs past that range. These checks confirm that impression reconciliation is a routine the publisher runs on purpose rather than a difference nobody is tracking.

audit_checks.title: The Magnite-versus-ad-server impression discrepancy is not measured against a set tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the SSP and the ad server disagree on impression counts and nobody is comparing the two against a tolerance, a drifting gap goes unnoticed. The side that counts fewer impressions is usually the side that pays, so an unwatched discrepancy can understate billable inventory or hide a delivery problem that is costing the publisher revenue.

educate.base:
A supply-side platform (SSP) such as Magnite reports the impressions it transacted, and the publisher's own ad server reports the impressions it recorded for the same inventory. These two numbers come from two systems that count at different moments in the same ad call, so they are expected to differ. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) and the Media Rating Council have moved the standard counting point to 'count on begin to render', meaning an impression counts once the ad starts to render on the page rather than the moment it is served. Two platforms that count at slightly different points along that path will land on slightly different totals for the same inventory.

A gap, then, is normal. The audit question is whether the publisher knows how large the gap is and whether they have decided what size of gap is acceptable. Industry practice treats a discrepancy under roughly 10 percent as ordinary and a discrepancy past that range as a signal to investigate, though the right tolerance depends on the inventory and the publisher should set it deliberately rather than borrow a number. Without a stated tolerance and a regular comparison, a discrepancy that drifts wider over time looks the same as one that has held steady for months.

Why the gap matters financially comes down to which side counts fewer impressions. Reconciliation usually settles billing on one agreed count, and the side reporting the lower number is usually the side that pays. If Magnite consistently counts fewer impressions than the ad server, the publisher may be recording inventory the SSP is not paying for. If the ad server counts fewer, the publisher's own reporting may understate what ran. Either direction is a number worth knowing.

A few signs point to a discrepancy that is not being measured against a tolerance:
  • No regular comparison exists between Magnite's impression report and the ad server's impression report for the same inventory and date range
  • The publisher cannot state what discrepancy percentage they treat as acceptable
  • Impression totals are read from each platform in isolation, with no reconciliation step that puts the two side by side
You may want to confirm that the discrepancy between Magnite and the ad server is measured on a regular cadence and compared against a tolerance the publisher set on purpose. One approach is to pull matched impression counts from both systems for the same inventory and window, calculate the percentage gap, and record whether it sits inside or outside the accepted range.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, impression volume can swing with traffic spikes around a major story or a seasonal peak, and a discrepancy that holds steady at low volume can widen during a surge. You may want to measure the gap across both quiet and high-traffic windows so the tolerance reflects the busiest days rather than an average that the quiet days pull down.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad inventory often sits alongside product and category pages that load heavier than editorial pages, and slower rendering can widen the gap between a served count and a begin-to-render count. You may want to compare the discrepancy on the commercial templates against the editorial ones since a page that renders ads later may show a larger gap.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, Joined by IAB Tech Lab and MMA, Announces Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed reporting documentation for matching its impression counts to a publisher ad server is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Pull matched impression counts from Magnite and the publisher's ad server for the same inventory and date range, calculate the percentage gap, and confirm whether a tolerance has been set and is being checked.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console reporting
steps: Open the Magnite console (the seller-facing reporting interface, which includes Demand Manager for header-bidding inventory) and build an impression report for the inventory under review.
Set the date range to a complete, recent window, for example the prior calendar month, and break the report out by the ad unit or placement you can match to the ad server.
Record Magnite's impression total for each matched slice of inventory.

ad_server_reporting.label: Publisher ad server reporting
steps: In the publisher's own ad server, build an impression report for the same inventory and the same date range used on the Magnite side.
Record the ad server's impression total for each matched slice, then calculate the percentage gap as the absolute difference divided by the larger of the two counts.

Ask the publisher what discrepancy percentage they treat as acceptable, and record whether each slice sits inside or outside that tolerance, along with whether this comparison is run on a regular cadence.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the Magnite and ad-server impression totals for matched inventory and date ranges, the calculated percentage gap for each, the tolerance the publisher set, and whether the comparison runs on a regular cadence. A setup where the discrepancy is measured routinely and each slice sits inside a stated tolerance passes. A setup where no regular comparison exists, or where no tolerance has been set, is a fail recorded with the inventory affected and the gap measured at audit time.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Magnite and ad-server impression totals, the percentage gap, and the publisher's tolerance above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not measure the impression discrepancy between Magnite and the ad server against a set tolerance, so a drifting gap between the two counts would go unnoticed. The two systems count at different points in the ad call, so a gap is expected, but the side that counts fewer impressions is usually the side that pays, and an unwatched gap can understate billable inventory or hide a delivery problem. {{voice_recommend}} pulling matched impression counts from Magnite and the ad server for the same inventory and window, calculating the percentage gap, and setting a tolerance the publisher checks on a regular cadence. After the cadence is in place, treat any slice that runs past the tolerance as a reconciliation item to investigate.

audit_checks.title: Discrepancy sources are not investigated when the gap runs out of range
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Measuring the discrepancy only helps if the publisher acts when it runs past tolerance. A gap that widens past the accepted range points to a fixable cause, for example a timeout, page latency, or a blocked creative, and leaving the cause uninvestigated turns a measurable signal into unrecovered revenue.

educate.base:
Knowing the impression discrepancy between Magnite and the ad server is the first half of reconciliation. The second half is doing something about it when the gap runs past the tolerance the publisher set. A discrepancy that drifts out of range is usually a symptom with a findable cause, and the same handful of causes account for most of the gap.

Timeouts are a frequent source on header-bidding inventory. Magnite's Demand Manager runs on the Prebid framework, and Prebid applies an auction timeout that discards any bid arriving after the deadline. The Prebid documentation recommends an internal auction timeout of 1,000 milliseconds or less, with an overall failsafe timeout of 3,000 milliseconds or less. A timeout set too tight discards bids that would have rendered, so Magnite may record demand the ad server never served, which widens the gap. Page latency works the same way. If the ad starts to render slowly, the count-on-begin-to-render moment arrives late or never, and the two systems diverge on whether the impression counted at all.

Counting method is a structural source rather than a fault. Two platforms that count at slightly different points along the ad call will always show a baseline gap, and that part of the discrepancy is not a problem to fix, only a constant to account for when setting the tolerance. Blocked creatives are a different matter. If a creative is blocked by the publisher's controls, an ad blocker, or a brand-safety filter after Magnite has counted it but before the ad server records a render, the SSP count exceeds the ad server count for that slice.

A few sources are worth ruling in or out when the gap runs past tolerance:
  • Auction or failsafe timeouts set tight enough to discard bids that would otherwise render
  • Page latency that delays the begin-to-render moment so the impression counts in one system and not the other
  • A difference in the counting point between Magnite and the ad server that sets a baseline gap
  • Creatives blocked after the SSP count but before the ad server render
You may want to confirm that an out-of-range discrepancy triggers an investigation into these sources rather than being logged and left. One approach is to check the timeout settings, the rendering path, and the creative-blocking controls for the affected inventory before accepting the gap as unavoidable.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, heavy article pages with embedded media can delay the begin-to-render moment for ad slots lower on the page, which widens the gap on those placements specifically. You may want to check whether the out-of-range slices cluster on the heaviest templates since that points the investigation at page latency rather than a timeout setting.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product pages often carry scripts for pricing, reviews, and personalization that compete with the ad render, and that contention can delay the begin-to-render moment. You may want to look at the rendering path on the commercial templates first when a discrepancy runs out of range since those pages tend to carry the most competing scripts.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, Joined by IAB Tech Lab and MMA, Announces Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's guidance on diagnosing impression discrepancies for a specific account is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: For any inventory where the discrepancy runs past tolerance, check the likely sources in turn, the timeout settings, the rendering path, the counting points, and the creative-blocking controls, and record which one accounts for the gap.

magnite_demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: Open Demand Manager and review the Prebid auction and failsafe timeout values applied to the out-of-range inventory.

Compare them against the Prebid recommendation of 1,000 milliseconds or less for the auction timeout and 3,000 milliseconds or less for the failsafe timeout, and note whether a tight timeout could be discarding bids that would otherwise render.
Record whether the timeout setting is a plausible source of the gap for the affected slices.

rendering_and_creative_review.label: Rendering and creative review
steps: Use the browser developer tools on a sample of the affected pages to watch how quickly the ad slot reaches the begin-to-render moment, and note any latency that delays or prevents the render.
Check the publisher's creative-blocking controls and any brand-safety filters to see whether creatives are blocked after Magnite counts them but before the ad server records a render.
Record which source, the timeout, page latency, the counting-point baseline, or a blocked creative, accounts for the out-of-range gap on each slice.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each slice where the discrepancy runs past tolerance, which source was checked and what it showed, covering timeout settings, page latency, the counting-point baseline, and blocked creatives. A setup where every out-of-range slice has a source investigated and either corrected or accounted for passes. A setup where out-of-range gaps are logged without any investigation into the cause is a fail recorded with the affected inventory and the gap measured.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which discrepancy source was checked for each out-of-range slice and what it showed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not investigate the source of an impression discrepancy when the gap between Magnite and the ad server runs past tolerance, so a fixable cause goes uncorrected. An out-of-range gap usually traces to a findable source, for example a tight auction timeout discarding bids, page latency delaying the begin-to-render moment, or a creative blocked after the SSP count. {{voice_recommend}} checking the timeout settings in Demand Manager, the rendering path on the affected pages, and the creative-blocking controls whenever a slice runs out of range, then correcting the cause or accounting for it. After each out-of-range gap has a source on record, the publisher can tell an unavoidable counting-point baseline apart from a problem worth fixing.
